article.title: Apple Turns 50 With Global Celebrations, Paul McCartney Concert, and a Nasdaq Bell Ringing From Apple Park
article.summary: Apple marks its 50th anniversary on April 1, 2026, with worldwide events, a private Paul McCartney concert at Apple Park, and a special animated homepage tribute to five decades of products.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Apple turned 50 on April 1, 2026, exactly half a century after Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne signed the partnership agreement that launched the company from a garage in Los Altos, California. The milestone was marked with a weeks-long series of events spanning 10 cities and three continents, culminating in a private Paul McCartney concert at Apple Park and a ceremonial Nasdaq opening bell ringing from the company's Cupertino headquarters.

## What We Know

CEO Tim Cook kicked off the formal celebration on March 12 with an open letter titled "50 Years of Thinking Different," published on [Apple's newsroom](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/apple-to-celebrate-50-years-of-thinking-different/). In the letter, Cook reflected on the company's trajectory from the Apple II and Macintosh through the iPod, iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, and Apple Vision Pro, emphasizing the company's longstanding commitment to combining technology with the liberal arts.

On March 31, Nasdaq brought its opening bell to Apple Park, where Cook rang it to formally open the anniversary day's trading session, according to [TechRadar](https://www.techradar.com/news/live/apple-50th-anniversary-celebration). That evening, Paul McCartney headlined a private concert for Apple employees at the company's headquarters. Cook introduced the former Beatle by noting that Steve Jobs "loved The Beatles" and "often said he built Apple to be like them," [TechRadar reported](https://www.techradar.com/news/live/apple-50th-anniversary-celebration). McCartney performed a 25-song set spanning his career with The Beatles and as a solo artist.

### Global Events

The celebration extended well beyond Cupertino. Beginning March 13, [Apple hosted events at flagship retail locations worldwide](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/apple-hosts-50th-anniversary-celebrations-around-the-world/), each pairing creative performances with Today at Apple sessions:

- **New York**: 17-time Grammy Award winner Alicia Keys performed at Apple Grand Central, with Tim Cook joining her on stage.
- **London**: Mumford & Sons played at Apple Battersea, alongside DJ Nia Archives.
- **Paris**: A pop-up recording studio at Apple Champs-Elysees featured French touch pioneers Breakbot and Cassius working with emerging artists.
- **Shanghai**: Fashion designer Feng Chen Wang presented a collection titled "Life and Love" during Shanghai Fashion Week at Apple Jing'an.
- **Tokyo**: Virtual artist Mori Calliope discussed her creative journey, which she said began by downloading songs onto an iPod nano.
- **Washington, D.C.**: Academy Award winner Troy Kotsur and Gallaudet University president Roberta Cordano discussed Deaf creativity at Apple Carnegie Library.
- **Sydney**: Digital artwork by 11 emerging Australian artists was projected onto the Sydney Opera House sails, accompanied by a score composed in Logic Pro.

### Homepage Tribute

On April 1 itself, Apple updated its homepage with a special animated video rendered in a sketch-art illustrative style, progressing through the company's most iconic products from the original Macintosh through the iMac, iPod, MacBook, AirPods, iPhone 17 Pro, and Vision Pro, as [reported by 9to5Mac](https://9to5mac.com/2026/04/01/apple-50-birthday-homepage-celebration/). The accompanying message read: "At 50 years, it's only natural to look back. But Apple has always looked forward."

Employees received commemorative gifts including a t-shirt, limited-edition poster, and enamel pin, according to [TechRadar](https://www.techradar.com/news/live/apple-50th-anniversary-celebration). Special installations at Apple Park showcased the complete evolution of the iMac lineup from the original G3 to the M4, alongside every iPhone model from the 2007 original through the iPhone 17 Pro Max and iPhone Air.

### Cook's Forward-Looking Message

In his anniversary letter, Cook pledged that Apple would continue to push forward on "groundbreaking silicon, transformative software and life-enriching products" while deepening commitments to environmental responsibility, education, and accessibility, according to [Apple's newsroom](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/apple-to-celebrate-50-years-of-thinking-different/). The company also announced an expansion of its partnership with Save The Music, nearly doubling the number of schools reached and aiming to provide music education to 25,000 students.

## What We Don't Know

Apple notably did not use the anniversary to announce any new products or services. Whether the company has timed any product announcements to follow the celebration week remains unclear. The company's next scheduled event window is WWDC 2026 in June, where Apple is widely expected to unveil the Core AI framework that would replace Core ML.

The broader strategic implications of the anniversary messaging also remain open. Cook's letter emphasized environmental responsibility, education, and accessibility as future priorities, but specific commitments beyond the Save The Music expansion have not been detailed.

## Analysis

Apple's decision to center its 50th anniversary around creative performances and community events rather than product launches reflects a deliberate branding choice. Where technology companies typically use milestones to announce new hardware or services, Apple opted to reinforce its identity as a company that enables human creativity, a narrative that dates to the original Macintosh's positioning as a tool for "the rest of us."

The choice of Paul McCartney as the headline act carries particular symbolic weight. Apple Corps, The Beatles' holding company, and Apple Inc. fought a decades-long trademark dispute that was finally settled in 2007. The two Apples' relationship has since become collaborative, with The Beatles' catalog arriving on iTunes in 2010. Having McCartney perform at Apple Park on the company's 50th birthday closes a circle that began with a lawsuit and ends with a concert.

At 50, Apple is the world's most valuable company, with a market capitalization that has at times exceeded $4 trillion. The anniversary arrives during a period of significant strategic transition, as the company integrates generative AI into its product line through Apple Intelligence while navigating regulatory pressure from the EU's Digital Markets Act and a U.S. Department of Justice antitrust case. Whether the next 50 years prove as transformative as the first will depend in large part on how Apple navigates these intersecting challenges.
